svg.text: Dijkstra — Greedy Relaxation From a Min-Heap Frontier
1. dist[s] = 0; dist[v] = ∞ for v ≠ s. Push (0, s) on min-heap.
2. Pop (d, u). If d > dist[u] continue (stale entry).
3. For each edge (u, v, w): if dist[u] + w < dist[v], relax: dist[v] = dist[u] + w; push (dist[v], v).
4. Repeat until heap empty (or pop target t for early exit).
Invariant on pop: the popped node's distance is final because all shorter paths would have
already been popped first — this requires non-negative edge weights.
Negative edges break the invariant. Use Bellman-Ford or Johnson's reweighting.
• Binary heap: O((V + E) log V). Default in std libraries; lazy-deletion of stale entries.
• Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V). Decrease-key in O(1) amortized — theoretically optimal,
practically slower than binary due to constants. Rarely used in production.
• Array / linear scan: O(V² + E). Wins on dense graphs (E ≈ V²) where log V overhead loses.
• Indexed/d-ary heaps: O((V + E) log_d V) — d=4 often beats binary on dense graphs.
• Dial's algorithm (bucket queue): O(V + E + maxWeight) for small integer weights.
Pick the structure to match graph density and edge-weight distribution.
